speaker: interviewer
text: Mary Stolar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, July 13, 1995. You want to continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Well, we moved into this little place-- little. It was a room and kitchen. And you can hear, behind the wall-- it was a very thin wall. The landlord did not know there is a Jewish woman with a child hiding. He rented it-- [? Kola ?] rented it for [INAUDIBLE]-- for him-- a man-- a young man. Now, going to-- this [? Kola, ?] he started to drink. Maybe he was claiming because of his leg. He started to drink. Most of the Polish-- it's a well-known fact, they are alcoholics, many of them, in a very young age. And more and more and more. And he was going to the bar. He came from the bar-- started to be extremely aggressive, and he started to beat me up. I was raped from him every night. I can't call it 'sex.' I became pregnant. And my son was terrified when he saw him. Mama, Mama, he put himself into me like he want to go inside my body-- he put himself. Innocently, so very scared, and so very-- such a gorgeous little child, like an angel with blond, very blond and blue-eyed, yeah. And I became pregnant. And my-- my brother-in-law, the non-Jew was [? wifed. ?] He was living, like, behind the ghetto. And his wife was-- well, his wife was hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing-- they was-- squealed on him. The Gestapo came. Now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, I missed a very important thing. I have to go back for a little while. Can I?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were going there, when the ghetto was finished, and I hide-- I went to my brother-in-law, there was already squealed on him neighbors that they knew he have a Jewish family. After his wife's-- Jewish family. They send up to a Gestapo man. I'll never forget this thing. There was I, my mother-in-law, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister-in-law, and two children-- one child. A boy, David. And we hide in-- they've got a little, small room-- it's like a storage room-- a dark, little room. My Brother-in-law's apartment was a nice one, but got a little, this was someplace for the cleaning woman. They were, financially, very nicely off. He was an engineer. And we were-- I'll never forget-- we went into this room, all of us, closed the door, and holding-- I was holding my son, and-- David, and he says, sh! Sh! Sh! We were all sitting there. The old lady was a little deaf. My mother-in-law was a little deaf. And then-- guard-- they walked-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], we hear them, going from room to room. And my brother-in-law was an excellent actor. In fact, he was not only Gentile, he was German Gentile-- from Latvia, from Riga. But he hated the Nazis, naturally, obviously, being married to a Jewish woman. And he spoke Yiddish excellent. His name was Rosenberg-- Edward Rosenberg. Well-- well-- OK. I go back to this, and they left. The second time, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they was-- they were hidden, the second time, without me. This is another part. They did catch the old lady. They took her out, screaming, and they killed her. The old lady disappeared before we-- they survived a little bit. Now, going back to this a minute. I became pregnant in the new apartment. I have to shush with my son to speak very-- he didn't realize. He would say, Mama. There was-- the windows was closed with-- not a window shade, an old bedsheet-- a dark one-- closed the window altogether.

speaker: interviewee
text: He will look through a little bit of the window. He will see children playing. He became so ecstatic. Mama, please, can I go out for a second, outside, Mama? And I said, no, you can't. Then he will ask for food, then he will ask going out. Then he will--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any toilet there. We have to have the-- our biological things-- 'physiological'-- whatever you call it-- in the kitchen, in a-- in a pail. And he was taking care of it, which I understand. But anyway, he came. He wouldn't chase me out, but he was-- beat me. And then I became pregnant. And I-- I say to my brother-in-law, [? Eddie, ?] please, I want to get rid of it. I want to get rid of it. We find a woman. She wasn't a doctor. The people-- woman-- people do abortions, not being doctors. He took me there to him, in the evening. I have to disguise myself-- put something on my face like I have a blind-- one eye-- to disguise, because somebody from the Polish will recognize me. And we take-- got an old coat, wearing. It was winter. And I was having, already, a little-- it was advanced the pregnancy, high. Now, he brought me to this woman. She examined me. She asked me questions. And she said-- two reasons-- I think she know that I'm Jewish. She didn't want to touch me. The second reason-- she said, it's very highly advanced, my pregnancy. And she-- forget about it. I went back, and I was disappointed and angry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] going on [INAUDIBLE] I said to myself, why am I sitting here? Why is not better to die? He was-- when I hear him open the door with the keys, and I was terrified. He walks in. Oh, god.

speaker: interviewee
text: In May-- I never forget-- May, the 11th May, I give birth to this child. He brought me in a woman-- find out about a woman who is-- what do you call this woman who helped-- not a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Midwife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Midwife. That's exactly. A very nice woman, happened to be. I don't know where he took her from. He brought-- he find a-- he asked somebody. She came in, and she said-- and she was not aware that I am not-- that I'm Jewish. She thought I am his girlfriend-- to be having an affair, and we want it so secretly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said to  him, sir, mister, they're so antihygienic, everything. Don't you have a few bedsheet-- something-- a blanket? How can I receive the child without any-- I started to get-- how do you call? To have the baby. The labor-- you went into labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor. Oh, god. And he stands behind this woman and making suggestions to me that-- not to cry, not to scream. You are a mother, and you know the pain. I don't know if [? Joanie's ?] a mother. Not yet. I don't have to tell you the labor, but-- And I have to bite my-- my-- my lips. I bite. I was having bitten here and-- and hold onto it, because it was-- now, these days, they're giving something against the pain. They give something-- that you-- you push, but you don't have the-- whatever. And I gave birth to this little child. And she needs hot water, and she was criticizing-- why don't you-- I don't-- because she didn't realize the situation. And I get-- gave birth to this beautiful little child. And she was such a good-- a very good measurement. And she was average-- not little, not-- And I gave her this breast, and she was nursing. And we made a pact that he's going to put her away in a nursing home-- in a findling home. I'll never forget when he took the child. The midwife left-- whatever. She didn't-- he didn't pay her, and she did it for being a Catholic-- good Christian-- whatever. I'll never forget when the child-- he told me-- being an invalid, and walking with a cane, couldn't he [INAUDIBLE]-- very fast, he was back, without her. Very, very fast! He couldn't go to the findling home. I know it was too far. And walking the way he walks. He was holding the cane and the other hand holding this little, beautiful child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I didn't know nothing anymore. We didn't talk about it, and I wouldn't even ask him. And I told him, did you put it there? Yes, I told you. Yes-- don't ask me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. By the way, my son wasn't with me. Another thing-- I have to go back. These few days, my brother-in-law-- my brother-in-law, the non-Jewish brother-in-law-- took him by-- took him to his wife, where she was hiding, in a little village. Got a good hiding place. They paid them, and they got food. This is the biggest [INAUDIBLE] was I, the one who was [INAUDIBLE]. Then he wasn't around, my son, with me. I was so glad he couldn't just be stuck-- stick to me like he did. And anyway, I recovered in a few days. And I wasn't thinking, even, because I was facing, the-- and the situation was-- I wasn't-- I must admit I wasn't thinking about the baby. And he was worse and worse and worse. Until this man find out-- Mr. [? Jelinski ?] find out that it was hidden. This is a Jewish woman there with a child, and he asked him to move out. He said, OK, I will find a place. Then the Germans were-- how do you say that, when-- oh my god, they start confiscating apartments. They needed it for the-- for the-- for the army. They put-- took out-- they didn't even ask. They didn't have to ask. We are taking this room. I want to be available this room, this apartment. And they were going to-- going to take it. Once, he walked in-- OK, they're going to take this apartment. One time, he opened the door with the keys. He walked in, with somebody-- a little girl-- a girl, bundled up in clothes. And I look at her, and she's [? Fela. ?] We knew each other. We were in the ghetto together.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a teenager, and I knew her parents. We [? around-- ?] happened to be in the same backyard together. You know? When we lived in the ghetto, their apartment was there in a compound, kind of.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, [? Fela, ?] what are-- oh my god, [? Fela. ?] She-- her parents were also killed in the [INAUDIBLE]-- no, in the ghetto, when they finished out the Jews from the [INAUDIBLE]. And her mother said, [? Fela, ?] go hide-- go away. Go away from. They were standing to be placed in the trucks. Go away-- go away-- go away. And she went to-- into a big garbage can, she stuffed herself. She survived. She went to a village, and she was there, and they find out, and she came back to the city, to Rowne. And she saw [? Kola. ?] She saw [? Kola. ?] And she knew him from before. He was coming there. They used to live-- this is such a coincidence. They used to live-- before the war, they used to live in a different street, and they-- they-- [? Kola ?] with his parents, and that's the reason she know him. And also she saw him coming to me in the ghetto, visiting me. And she saw him on the street, and she said, [? Kola, ?] please-- And he says, [? Fela! ?] Mary's there with me, with the baby. Come, come. And he took here there. We were there and he took her around. When he was going terrible-- terribly drunk, and she said, Mary, how can you stay-- Mary, I would prefer to go out and die. Anyway, once, they were going to confiscate the apartment. They are going to knock the door any minute. We hide, all three of us-- [? Fela, ?] myself, and my son-- under the bed. We were sitting-- lying there, under the bed. And she became hysterical, and she wanted to laugh. This is such a sickness. And I said, [? Fela, ?] please. [LAUGHS] She was choking laughter-- such a hysterical. And, thank god, we survived. After, I was so angry with her. And she said, I'm sorry, Marry. She's in Israel. She survived. [? Fela ?] survived. I hope she's not-- she's-- she's about seven years younger than I-- seven, eight. She's married, with children. She has second husband-- that's another story. Now-- she has two sons. Yes. The first husband was Sephardic. The second is Ashkenazi. She has two sons, one with him and one with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, after they-- we survived, all three of us. We expected any minute they'll come back to confiscate the apartment. We were told they confiscate the apartment. Then I didn't know where to go. I just didn't know what to do! I didn't know where to go. And I said, I'm going to be killed, and I don't care, and they're going to take me out of the house, and-- he said, no, you're not! You-- this is going to be my debt also. I want you to live. And maybe you'll find something. You can-- Now listen, listen, listen, listen. Once-- OK, this is another episode. He walked in, [? Kola, ?] and said, Mary, these are a couple, a Jewish couple, without children. Elderly. They have money. They are going to pay me, and I have to take them in. Because we had no money to live on. They will find you, they will find them, too. And the name was [? Fischel. ?] He name was [? Gooz, ?] their last name [? Gooz. ?] And they survived. The wife was kind of half-blind in one eye. She was wearing big glasses. They never got any children. They walked in, and we were sitting, all-- all of us. We were already two, and two-- four of us. Later, again, like I said, they're going to confiscate. Then they were a big part, and I don't know how much money they gave him. And we are starting out to having a little more food. And-- they were-- Before the war, they were wealthy people, these [? Fischel ?] [? Gooz. ?] [? Fischel. ?] We called them [? Fischel Gooz ?] with his wife. Later, they are going to confiscate again. They were afraid. Their landlord told them to move out. This is-- I jump from things to thing. And they left, this couple. The Jewish elderly couple left. They will find something, because they ask him to-- asked them to leave. I took [? Erik-- ?] I took my son-- I called him [? Erik ?] now. And I'll never forget this thing. I'll never forget it. I don't know where to go and what to go. My-- my-- my brother-in-law, the non-Jewish, wasn't even at home. He was lately-- he want to get rid of this obligation. [INAUDIBLE] wife. And the other-- and the child of another sister. [INAUDIBLE] and his wife. They were his-- his responsibility. Then I'll never forget, I walked out of the house, and I went into a toilet. And this toilet was so primitive. It's not like water-- it's not like-- you know, a different toilet. What I mean-- I don't know-- you people ever read about it. This is something very primitive. It's never flushed. There is no construction. There is not a flush-- nothing to flush. This is the way it was in this place where I came from, even before the war. I know Americans couldn't imagine things like that-- the poor people, because this construction-- these things cost a lot-- the instruction in-- in-- to put things and all the-- the work-- is only for the wealthy one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there with my son. And we were until here and-- excuse my expression-- in-- in human stew. And I say, where am I going to go? Where am I going to go? And then I went to Mr.-- What is his name? I just was there lying in bed, and I said, wait a minute, I forgot the name. This was an engineer, a friend of my non-Jewish brother-in-law. He used to come for dinners there, on the Sundays. He was a nice man. He was, still, nice-- objective. He wouldn't do nothing maybe good for Jewish, but he wouldn't do nothing bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know him where he lived. He was all alone in an apartment. And I went all the way-- first, I started my-- my brother-in-law. The door was locked up. We were smelly. And I went back to the [NON-ENGLISH], we call this, our neighborhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to him, and I rang the door. And he says, what-- who are you? What do you-- And I said, sir, do you remember me? I am the sister-in-law of Mr. Rosenberg. I'm in trouble, and this and that. And I told him. And he said, come in, come in!

speaker: interviewee
text: And not far away were German-- Germans-- German-- Germans-- army-- officers. And-- and he gave me-- there was one room and a kitchen. The kitchen is a very short-- a very narrow couch-- a little couch. Lie down with-- lie down with my son. He was so tired and so smelly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, what is it? He says, there is water there. Would you go wash-- God bless him. I mean, he's not alive. He was old [INAUDIBLE]. But it was human of him. We sleeped over there. And he said, Mary, I am sorry, you have to go. I said, OK, Mr.-- what was his name? Well, anyway. He told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, if I go, I'm going to leave very, very early when nobody's on the streets empty. I went. I took my child. I went back to [? Kola. ?] I was lying-- I took-- I went into the yard, and I left my son out on the grass to find out if he's at home. I go to the door. I knock the door. He's not! He probably was drinking someplace. And when I-- I waited and waited, and I saw him coming. On the grass, we were both lying, and I said, god, he's going to catch a cold-- he's going to catch a cold-- pneumonia. And I will catch-- It was so wet, the grass.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then he came. And he said, my god, what are you doing here? What are you doing? And-- and I told him about Mr. Whatever. And then we stayed there again, again, until we have to give up this time, completely. Then I took my son-- chased me out, middle of the night-- you know what he did? Oy. One night, we spent-- two nights-- one night I spend at my brother-in-law's. The non-- the non-Jewish brother-in-law. I'll never forget it. I took out a robe. I took-- I washed myself. There was no shower, no bath. In spite, it was a nice apartment, it still wasn't so like it should be.

speaker: interviewee
text: I washed myself a little bit, and I put on a robe-- a robe in the closet. I figure I'll put it back. He wasn't there-- my brother-in-law. And I put my hand into the pocket, and there is a little bag lying there-- a bag. I pulled it out, and guess what. It was full of-- of golden dollars-- golden dollars. Was very, very expensive. A golden dollar. Golden-- pure golden! You heard about this currency. And I put it back there in the-- I was so naive and so stupid. When I came back to this [? Kola, ?] there, because I-- I was only-- he told me you can sleep here only one or two nights, Mary-- my brother-- it was-- it should have been-- it wasn't nice of him, but-- well, anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went. I went back to Kola. I told him about this little bag. Oh god. Why? When I [INAUDIBLE] to my son is Mama, how-- you, it's being so sensible and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I take this little bag, I could go away in another city and rent a room for this kind of money. Well, anyway. Don't you think he took away a little bit, and it's something you open like a lock. He went, he took everything and stole-- he went there. He stole-- he took this little bag. He gave something to his sister, the one who's in Auschwitz, if she's still alive. And the rest of it, he drank it over and chased me out. That's when we became-- I became really homeless-- homeless. This is a joke. With this child. And I say, I'm not going to be able anymore to survive. I'm going to have to go straight to the Gestapo and say, here I am, here we are, both Jews. Take me. I went to the railroad station. And I figure I've got to run away from this city where I was-- there is not written on my forehead that I'm Jewish. I have to get out of this damned Rowne. Then I went to the railroad station. There were so many soldiers. It was war then. Many soldiers traveling back and forth. You know-- so many army men. And I-- I went into the car-- to the-- to the train-- goes to Lwow. Lwow is a very big city in Poland. 'Luh-VOOV,' called, in Poland. Lwow. 'Lemberg,' in German. This is a city where there were universities. We used to hear of this city. I never been there, but I heard, and I figure-- And I also hear something about the Polish committee. There is lots of Polish refugees-- goyim-- Gentile. They were killed from the Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE]. You see, you've got to know-- to understand all this-- The Ukrainians were with-- working with the Germans. They became squealers. They became cooperating with them. And they hated the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] are Polish Catholics. They were-- they were going around killing not Jews but the Polacks from the small cities, and they were refugees going there. And I attach myself to those Polish refugees. Was a very good idea, in fact. I didn't look-- but I didn't have ID-- you've got to have a-- a birth certificate, a passport, or something, and I never got anything. Then I arrived in Lwow. I came to Lwow by myself, with my son, first of all. Before I find this Jewish-- this Polish center. I will never forget-- I-- we went to a park. It was a beautiful day in a Sunday-- people coming from church. I went in a park and fall right away asleep. My son right away asleep in a sitting park. And I let him sleep. After this, I looked around the beautiful buildings. I'll never forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a place in the early morning. I waited a little while, because it was too early. And I knocked the door. A woman came out. And she said, oh, who are you? In Polish. Who are-- what-- what-- what are you doing here? Who are you? She was scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, ma'am, I am not going to eat you. I am a Polish refugee. I just came from Rowne. I ran away. My husband and my father all were killed when the Ukrainians-- I made up the story. And it was a well-known fact. But other ones. It was a good excuse. Oh there is-- oh, then she became nice, and she said, oh, there is a committee, Polski committee. But it's Sunday. And I says, I know, I know, ma'am, but I don't-- we don't have place. She said, come in. We walked in, and she gave us breakfast. A nice apartment. She made some soup. And she said, this poor child is-- how could you, and this and that. And I made up a story how it was happening in Rowne. And she-- and we waited.

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept over until Monday morning. She took me with the strassenbahn. You know strassenbahn? The one-- rides in San Francisco? What do you-- Streetcar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Streetcar. She took me there to the Polish committee. And she gave me some-- a few-- a little change-- money. She wished me luck, and she said if I wouldn't work out nothing [? again, ?] she reported me to the-- she told these-- these-- the workers there, this is a refugee. She's very tired, and this little child, and take care of her. And she left. And she told me, there is no telephone business. She said if worse comes to worst, I can come back to her. She gave me some change for the tram back. And then I waited, and I registered. I registered. Sitting people, filling out forms. I go up with my [INAUDIBLE]. Instead of Stolar, I took Stolarska. Instead of Mary, I took Maria. And I still didn't have any documents. And-- and then I-- I said my-- my husband was a policemen. And they killed the policemen. They were very angry at the Polish policemen. He was taken out of the house, and he was killed. And my family are Polish patriots, and here I am. And there was quite a few now, That's the reason I have this excellent excuse. Right away, they gave us some food. They were from the nuns-- nuns, working, volunteer-- doing volunteer work. They give us food. They gave-- they send me also a place where to sleep. No-- they say they are going to. They are going to send me a place where to spend the nights. Now I fall asleep, myself, on the backyard. It was a nice day. Fall asleep. And they-- my son told me that the lady was-- wake up, wake up. Somebody, somehow find out-- suspected-- that I'm Jewish. Somehow, somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: I woke up, and the woman there, sitting there with  her husband, he says, where are you from Rowne? Where did you go? Where is your parafia? 'Parafia' is the Catholics worked in the church-- whatever. Anyway. Now the form-- they want-- they filled out, I have to wait a few day. It goes to the German quarters. And they put a stamp, and they approved of it. You understand? I have to give them a little picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made an [? automat. ?] I wish I can have it-- I said to my friends, I always say, if I can't have those pictures, those four, those-- replacing a passport-- something-- like-- the ID. Anyway. And when they suspect-- and I said please, God, let me stick around for a few days and let me take this ID, and I'll be the happiest person in the world. And then they didn't bother me. I was sleeping [INAUDIBLE]. We were sleeping all. They didn't find place to sleep. They gave us some rooms-- straw-- some old things to cover. And we slept, all of us. And the nuns gave us some food. And that's the way we functioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a few days, I go upstairs to the office. And I was prepared they will say, Stolarska? Mrs. Stolarska? You are not-- you are not Polish. I was prepared for the worst. But thank God, they asked me to put the finger-- one finger and one-- signing the thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave me this-- this paper. I'll never forget this paper. When somebody asked me $1 million, I wouldn't give it away. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it's starting now, trouble in Lemberg-- in Lwow. Starting-- oh, they started to suspect me. One woman come over to me and said, listen-- listen, Mrs. Stolarska. Do you want we should take the baby to a [POLISH]? [POLISH] means a nuns' home. What do you call it? Convent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Convent. I said, no, of course not. And she said, Mrs. Stolarska, we know very well you are not Polish. You are not Catholic. I mean, we want to save the baby. And I say to myself, Mary, you'd better get the hell out of there. Now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I took-- oy, it's so many episodes. Because I was twice in Lemberg-- twice, twice, twice. I took my child. And I went-- OK, yes, yes, yes. I went to look for a-- for a place where I can sleep and I can help out. I find that place-- superintendent. There was an old house. Two couples. One couple, elderly people without children. To me, they were elderly. I was in my early-- early 20s.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, can I rent from you a corner of the-- of the place? And I will try to pay you people. They rented it to me. And I tried to work out someplace to go to work. And they said there is work for the Germans in a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I couldn't leave the child. They didn't want them to take the child with me. I wouldn't dare. Every minute when he went, I asked him, Lieber, where should-- we want [? pepe? ?] A thousand times a day, I ask him if he wants [? pepe. ?] And he was so sick of it. He says, Mama, I don't want any [? pepe. ?] Anyway, we went-- I went to the hospital. I worked a few days. I left my son with them. And I was terrified. And I just gave up-- gave up. And I didn't go anymore to them. Now, the bombs were falling every day-- started to fall-- bombing Lemberg. Which is Lwow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then-- then once-- I'll never forget this-- my god-- I took him in. I walked with him out in the park, where the people were starting to scream. We went in a basement, and there were people hiding from the bombs. And I thought, I wish a bomb falls down and I finish up here in this basement. But anyway, they-- they damaged-- narrowly a house, they damaged it. Narrowly, where I was sleeping with the superintendant-- two people. They damaged this house, and it couldn't be possible to sleep there. Then I find a little place in another place with an elderly Polish lady. I left [? Erik ?] there. I left my son there. And I'll never forget-- I go to pick up the few shmattes I've got. And she gave me-- somebody gave me-- I don't remember. Oh, I know-- [? Anna. ?] [? Anna. ?] She gave me a little jar with a little potato soup, for [? Erik. ?] He left-- I left him with the old lady over there. And I took this little jar. And I go with this little jar [INAUDIBLE]. And I have a little shmatte, a few shmattes here. I go to the tramway. And suddenly-- oy, god, I'll never forget it. Suddenly a Ukrainian came over to me, with a Ukrainian uniform, a Ukrainian uniform-- to me-- come.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's stop here, and you'll continue--
